---
title: "3.2 Bollinger Bands"
subtitle: "Video lesson slides"
author: "Algorithmic Trading"
notes: |
  The linear strategy from Chapter 2 is elegant but operationally reckless:
  exposure keeps growing as deviations grow. Bollinger bands turn the same mean
  reversion logic into a bounded yes-or-no rule with explicit entry and exit
  thresholds.

format:
  revealjs:
    theme: default
    css: ../../../../styles/reveal.css
    slide-number: true
    controls: true
    progress: true
    menu: false
    transition: slide
    transition-speed: fast
    center: false
    auto-stretch: true
    auto-animate: true
    show-notes: false
    scrollable: true
    embed-resources: false
filters:
  - ../../../../scripts/lua/remove-reveal-notes.lua
include-before-body:
  - ../../../../styles/reveal-audience-only.html
execute:
  echo: true
---

## Bollinger bands make linear mean reversion tradable by capping exposure at one unit.

| Linear strategy        | Bollinger strategy                   |
| ---------------------- | ------------------------------------ |
| position grows with Z  | position is 0, +1, or -1             |
| no bound on capital    | capital is easy to budget            |

::: {.notes}
Start from the practical failure of the unconstrained linear rule. It may be
useful for demonstrating an edge, but it is hard to allocate capital against a
strategy with no predefined exposure ceiling.
:::

## Entry and exit Z-scores turn one deviation measure into two decisions.

```text
enter long  when z < -entryZ
exit long   when z >= -exitZ
enter short when z >  entryZ
exit short  when z <=  exitZ
```

::: {.notes}
Keep the visible slide on signal logic, not code plumbing. The key shift is
that the Z-score is no longer directly proportional to size. It is now a
decision trigger.
:::

## The lookback can still come from half-life, even though the rule is now threshold based.

| Quantity             | Role                                |
| -------------------- | ----------------------------------- |
| lookback             | estimate mean and volatility        |
| entryZscore          | decide when deviation is large enough |
| exitZscore           | decide when reversion is sufficient |

::: {.notes}
This slide keeps continuity with Chapter 2. We are not abandoning the half-life
logic; we are keeping it for the time scale while adding two practical control
knobs for entry and exit.
:::

## The GLD-USO example shows that bounded trading can outperform pure scaling.

| Strategy compared     | Reported chapter result              |
| --------------------- | ------------------------------------ |
| linear spread rule    | lower APR and Sharpe                 |
| Bollinger band rule   | better APR and Sharpe in example     |

::: {.notes}
Close by showing the operational payoff. The more practical rule is not merely
easier to risk-manage; in Chan's example it also improves the backtest.
:::
